Original caption: "ELECTRIC CARS FOR HAULING MAIL. Chicago Post Office Subway." The 2-foot (610mm) gauge Illinois Tunnel Company U.S. Mail car is on tracks the platform of Grand Central Station in front of a Pere Marquette U.S. Mail car, with an elevator down into the freight tunnels in the middle background. The tunnel mail car is a Bettendorf convertable car, see William P. Bettendorf's U.S. Patents 1,036,786, 1,039,638, 1,062,689 and 1,032,348 for drawings and descriptions of the car. Location inferred by comparison with other photos of the train shed interior, maps of the tunnel, and Google's photos of the former station site.

The Pekin Theatre (also called Pekin Inn or Pekin Café) was located at 2700 State Street, Chicago, Illinois in the 1910s-1921. It was purported to be the first Chicago Theater owned and operated by African Americans. Many early jazz artists played there, most notably Joe "King" Oliver. This photo is courtesy of the Illinois Humanities Council and was published in the Chicago Reader (https://www.chicagoreader.com/Bleader/archives/2017/06/15/an-evening-at-the-pekin-theatre-re-creates-the-countrys-first-black-owned-music-hall). The upstairs section was called the Beaux Arts Café. Permanently closed by the police in 1920.

photos.caption: The Chicago skyline on Lake Michigan, photographed from Adler Planetarium in September 1970. The main landmarks are One First National Plaza (left, now Chase Tower), Prudential Building (center, now One Prudential Plaza) and John Hancock Center (right), A taller building, Two Prudential Plaza, was built next to the Prudential Building. Sears Tower had not yet been built. This photo is geotagged with the camera location.

photos.caption: Navy Pier from the Prudential Building. It was built in 1914-1916, and is about 3,300 ft (1,000 m) long. When I was young, people said that it was a mile (1.6 km) long. It was a Chicago city pier, and apparently never an official Navy facility.

Looking south on State Street, circa 1947. Across the street from the State and Lake “El” Station is the State-Lake Theatre, a major venue for RKO in Chicago. After closing on June 28, 1984, the State-Lake Theatre’s interior was gutted and converted into studios and offices for the ABC network’s Chicago affiliate, WLS-TV. The Chicago Theatre was placed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1979 and designated as a historic Chicago landmark on January 28, 1983. After an extensive restoration the theatre reopened in 1986 with a performance by Frank Sinatra. The small 606 seat Telenews opened on December 23, 1939 featuring a couple of cartoons, a newsreel, a comical short, and “March of Time” news reports, all in a single hour. It later became the Loop originally offering first run films and later B movies. The Loop Theater closed April 2, 1978.

photos.caption: Image of Dr. Wilfred Osgood and Julius Friesser standing in a room with crates full of animal skeletons, skulls, and horns in the Field Museum, located in Grant Park in the Loop community area of Chicago, Illinois, inspecting the artifacts. The specimens were collected during a field expedition to Abyssinia (now known as Ethiopia), sponsored by the Chicago Daily News and the Field Museum, in which Osgood and Friesser took part.

View of South LaSalle Street, looking north from West Jackson Boulevard, showing the Rookery Building at 209 South LaSalle Street in the Loop community area of Chicago, Illinois. Pedestrians are walking along sidewalk. Automobiles and horse drawn vehicles are on the street.

Dr. John B. Murphy standing in an entrance of Mercy hospital. Theodore Roosevelt was taken to Mercy Hospital after being shot on October 14, 1912 before a campaign speech in Milwaukee, Wisconsin. Mercy Hospital was located at 2537 South Prairie Avenue in the Near South Side community area of Chicago, Illinois.

Elevated view of spectators standing on the steps and the sidewalk in front of the Chicago Art Institute, watching vehicles parading down South Michigan Avenue watching the Armistice Day peace celebration parade. The Art Institute is located at 111 South Michigan Avenue in the Loop community area of . The Railway Exchange, 80 East Jackson Street, Orchestra Hall, 220 South Michigan Avenue and the Pullman Building, 79 East Adams Street are visible across the street.

Portrait of Hyde Park High School baseball team. Samuel "Sam" Ransom, an African American player, is sitting in the middle of the team. This photo has been cropped and touched up by me. This photonegative taken by a Chicago Daily News photographer may have been published in the newspaper. Cited as: SDN-001546, Chicago Daily News negatives collection, Chicago History Museum. Library of Congress and Chicago History Museum list this picture as 1903, but a 1918 article by Chicago Tribune writer Walter Eckersall says the two attended school together at Hyde Park from 1899 to 1902.

Hyde Park High School (Preparatory School) baseball team, eleven members, wearing uniforms, sitting in three rows outdoors on the field in Chicago, Illinois. Samuel "Sam" Ransom, an African American player, is sitting in the middle of the team. This photonegative taken by a Chicago Daily News photographer may have been published in the newspaper. Cited as: SDN-001546, Chicago Daily News negatives collection, Chicago History Museum. The Library of Congress and Chicago History Museum post the date of the picture as 1903, however fellow teammate and future writer for the Chicago Tribune, Walter Eckersall, writes in 1918 that the two attended high school together at Hyde Park from 1899 to 1902. So I believe the picture is 1902.

Five members of the Hyde Park High School football team in position to begin a play on an athletic field in the Hyde Park community area of Chicago, Illinois. photos.attribution: Larry Syverson from Richmond, VA, USA
photos.caption: The Sears Tower (now the Willis Tower) when completed in 1973 was the tallest building in the world. It remained the tallest building for 25 years. My family visited the observation deck in July 1984. The view was spectacular. The view became even cooler as a fog rolled in off Lake Michigan, allowing only the tops of some skyscrapers to show. My sons, Brent and Brian, is at the window looking to the north and east toward the John Hancock Tower (with the two antenna). July 21, 1984

photos.caption: Looking north from the Pittsfield Building toward John Hancock Center in 1970. You can see part of the Wrigley Building and Tribune somewhat closer, among taller but less well known buildings. I had an ear operation in Chicago in 1970, and returned for a number of checkups. The doctor's office was on a high floor of the Pittsfield Building, on E. Washington Street. It is a 38-story building, whiich was completed in 1927. It is 551 ft (168 m) high.

This is an 1890's view of the Grant Park area looking north, showing how it appeared when it was known as Lake Park and cluttered with rail yards and tents. photos.caption: Richard Nixon, U.S. Republican nominee for President, smiles while standing on his motorcade car and giving a "victory" sign during a ticker-tape parade northbound on S. LaSalle St. at W. Monroe St. in the Chicago Loop, September 4, 1968, a week after the rival Democratic National Convention was held in Chicago. His wife Pat Nixon sits on the back of the car and waves to the crowd, her feet in the back seat. In the background at the south end of the street, the Chicago Board of Trade Building.

photos.attribution: Photo by Richard C. Drew. Uploaded by Rickdrew at en.wikipedia
photos.caption: This was shot in the Englewood Shopping Center (pedestrian mall), Chicago, Illinois, Circa 1974, during the Back to School Parade. On stage are members of the Englewood Business Men's Association (EBMA), including (far left) John Simpson, Audrey Drew (woman, far right) and Richard Drew (other side of the man holding the mic.) In the 1970's, the EBMA had a goal of involving the shopping center with the community on several levels. Part of this program were frequent events, such as the Back To School Parade.
Prairie Shores buildings on South Side of Chicago, December 13, 1978

McDonald's, Hyde Park, Chicago. Looking NW to 1344 E. 53rd St. This McDonald’s was built between 1975 and 1978, because it wasn’t in the 1975 telephone directory. Note the sign "over 25 billion served." These signs started proclaiming millions, (I think) earlier in the 1970s, then graduated to billions. Store owners updated the amount. At some unknown time McDonald's stopped counting.
A southbound Englewood-Howard train emerging from the 13th Street Portal after departing Roosevelt Station. Train location is approximate.

Row houses on South Side of Chicago, 1978. Probably Drexel Boulevard near File:22 121278 Dayton Currency Exchange Chicago.jpg, which depicts the 3900 block of South Drexel. Both images have a bench in the same place (on the Drexel Boulevard median).
Based on the setting sun, I think this image is looking west from the south side of Promontory Point, Chicago.
Looking west on 53rd Street in Hyde Park, Chicago, after the 1979 Chicago Blizzard of January 13-14, 1979. Berman’s Hardware is 1377 E. 53rd St..

Looking NE across Michigan Avenue to 142 E Ontario, Near North Side, Chicago, 1979. The neighborhood had recently begun to gentrify, signaled by the modern font of the shop Whitehouse and Hardy and the art gallery on the second floor above it.
People shoveling snow in Chicago, January 1979. Looking south on Michigan Avenue on the Near North Side. Charmets is 750 N. Michigan, west side of the street.
Entrance to American Bank Note Company, 120 E. Cullerton, Near South Side, Chicago, 1979. Building is preserved and used as Bank Note Lofts.

View of water intake crib on Lake Michigan at Lawrence Avenue in the Uptown community area of Chicago, Illinois.

This map of the Chicago is color coded by ward. The map shows the city just two year after the great Chicago Fire of 1871. Each street is labeled and all railroad lines are noted throughout the map. Park and boulevards are colored in green and the reaches of the city is outlined in red. A line extending northeast off the coast delineates the tunnel to the fresh water intake crib. The map identifies “Soldier’s Graves,” (Confederate POWs) near Lincoln Park, which were earlier noted as “Rebel Graves.” The monument to Senator Stephen Douglas and his grave site is also noted at his former property at 35th and Lake. Railroads and waterways converge at the mouth of the Chicago River and resulted in what was once the artery for goods and raw materials to pass though from the eastern states to the ever-developing western frontier. This convergence is what was responsible for Chicago’s enormous growth from a small mid-western town to a national and eventually international powerhouse city of commerce and trade.

photos.caption: The Merchandise Mart was opened in 1930, after three years of construction. It was the largest building in the world when it was opened. It has 15 floors (20 in the tower). Floor area is about 4 million sq.ft. (380,000 sq.m.). About half of its space is devoted to wholesale showrooms. The remainder is retail showrooms, offices, a shopping mall, a hotel, restaurants, college classrooms - you name it, The view is from Marina City, looking west. The Chicago River is at left.

photos.caption: Buckingham Fountain is a Chicago Landmark in Grant Park. It was dedicated in 1927. My wife Eileen is in the left foreground. Eileen and I went to Chicago from time to time when we lived in Peoria. Our trips usually had something to do with shopping at Marshall Field's. The Standard Oil Building is under construction in the center of the photo. When completed in 1973 (the year after this photo), it would be 1,136 ft (346 m) high, and have 83 floors. It was built as the headquarters of Standard Oil Co. of Indiana. The name was changed to Amoco Center when the company changed its name. The building has since been sold, and is named Aon Center after its principal tenant, a financial company. John Hancock Center is in the background.

German pavilion (or German House) at Chicago's 1893 World's Columbian Exposition. It is modelled after the old town hall of Rothenburg ob der Tauber in Franconia, Germany. It remained standing after the end of the Expo. BLACKS AND WHITES TAKE TO THE WATER AT A 12TH STREET BEACH ON LAKE MICHIGAN ON CHICAGO'S SOUTH SIDE IN THE SUMMER OF 1973. FROM 1960 TO 1970 THE PERCENTAGE OF CHICAGO BLACKS WITH AN INCOME OF $7,000 OR MORE JUMPED FROM 26 TO 58%. MEDIAN BLACK INCOME DURING THE PERIOD INCREASED FROM $4,700 TO $7,883, BUT THE DOLLAR GAP BETWEEN THEIR GROUP AND THE WHITES ACTUALLY WIDENED. CHICAGO HAS 29 MILES OF LAKEFRONT AND 30 PUBLIC BEACHES

Original Caption: Blacks And Whites Take To The Water At A 12th Street Beach On Lake Michigan On Chicago's South Side In The Summer Of 1973. From 1960 To 1970 The Percentage Of Chicago Blacks With An Income Of $7,000 Or More Jumped From 26 To 58%. Median Black Income During The Period Increased From $4,700 To $7,883, But The Dollar Gap Between Their Group And The Whites Actually Widened.
